---
title: "The Central Limit Theorem (CLT)"
output:
  beamer_presentation:
    theme: "default"
    colortheme: "default"
    fonttheme: "default"
  ioslides_presentation: default
  slidy_presentation: default
header-includes:
- \usepackage{enumerate}
- \usepackage{hyperref}
- \usepackage{caption}
- \usepackage[normalem]{ulem}
- \definecolor{links}{HTML}{2A1B81}
- \hypersetup{colorlinks,linkcolor=,urlcolor=links}
colorlinks: true
linkcolor: blue
urlcolor: blue
---

```{r, echo = FALSE}
library(knitr);
opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE);
set.seed(20260210);
```




## Normal distribution 

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A plot of a bell curve, shaded in blue and centered at the x-axis on a value of 7 is shown.", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
xx       <- seq(from = -5, to = 15, by = 0.001);
pr_norm  <- dnorm(x = xx, mean = 7, sd = 1);
plot(x = xx, y = pr_norm, type = "l", ylim = c(0, 0.48), 
     ylab = "Probability density",  xlab = "x", lwd = 3, xlim = c(4, 10),
     cex.lab = 2, cex.axis = 2, yaxs = "i");
polygon(c(xx, max(xx)), c(pr_norm, 0), col="blue");
```



## Sampling from a population (distribution)

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "30+ bird silhouettes in a dotted box called 'population'. Within this, four birds are in a grey box called 'sample'.", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/population_sample_1.png")
```



## Consider the distribution of a sample of N = 40


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "40 bird silhouettes in a grey box called 'sample'.", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/just_sample.png")
```



## Consider the distribution of a sample of N = 40

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A uniform distribution is shown, which appears as a blue rectangle in the middle of a plot with 'wing length (cm)' on the x-axis and 'Probability' on the y-axis", fig.cap = "Distribution of a sample of bird wing length (cm)", out.width="100%"}
NN          <- 40;
wing_length <- runif(n = NN, min = 5, max = 9);
for(i in 1:NN){
  if(wing_length[i] < 7 & wing_length[i] > 6){
    wing_length[i] <- wing_length[i] + runif(n = 1, min = -1, max = 0);
  }
  if(wing_length[i] < 8 & wing_length[i] > 7){
    wing_length[i] <- wing_length[i] + runif(n = 1, min = 0, max = 1);
  }
}
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
hist(x = wing_length, col = "blue", cex.axis = 2, cex.lab = 2, main = "",
     xlab = "Wing length (cm)");
```



## Sample means always distributed normally around true mean

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Grey boxes each with 4 birds representing samples with different average heights.", fig.cap = "", out.width="95%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/samples_wing_40.png")
```





## Sample means always distributed normally around true mean

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Grey boxes each with 4 birds representing samples with different average heights.", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/samples_wing.png")
```

\LARGE


What if we had 1000 sample means?


## Sample means always distributed normally around true mean


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A histogram showing normally distributed data centered at 7.", fig.cap = "Distribution of the mean of bird wing length (cm) samples (N = 40), with sampling repeated 1000 times.", out.width="90%"}
iters    <- 1000;
NN       <- 40;
the_mean <- NULL;
for(j in 1:iters){
  wing_length <- runif(n = NN, min = 5, max = 9);
  for(i in 1:NN){
    if(wing_length[i] < 7 & wing_length[i] > 6){
      wing_length[i] <- wing_length[i] + runif(n = 1, min = -1, max = 0);
    }
    if(wing_length[i] < 8 & wing_length[i] > 7){
      wing_length[i] <- wing_length[i] + runif(n = 1, min = 0, max = 1);
    }
  }
  the_mean[[j]] <- mean(wing_length);
}
all_means <- unlist(the_mean);
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
hist(x = all_means, col = "blue", cex.axis = 2, cex.lab = 2, main = "",
     xlab = "Sample mean of Wing length (cm)", breaks = 20,
     xlim = c(6.25, 7.75), ylim = c(0, 140));
mean_height <- 7;
arrows(x0 = mean_height, x1 = mean_height,
       y0 = 20, y1 = 1, lwd = 5, col = "red");
mbox <- function(x0, x1, y0, y1){
    xx <- seq(from=x0, to=x1, length.out = 100);
    yy <- seq(from=y0, to=y1, length.out = 100);
    xd <- c(rep(x0, 100), xx, rep(x1,100), rev(xx));
    yd <- c(yy, rep(y1,100), rev(yy), rep(y0, 100));
    return(list(x=xd, y=yd));
}
tbox <- mbox(x0 = mean_height - 0.2, 
             x1 = mean_height + 0.2, y0 = 20, y1 = 40);
polygon(x=tbox$x, y=tbox$y, lwd=3, border="red", col="white");
text(x = mean_height, y = 34, cex = 2, labels = "Population");
text(x = mean_height, y = 26, cex = 2, labels = "Mean");
```



## Sample means always distributed normally around true mean


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A histogram showing normally distributed data centered at 7.", fig.cap = "Distribution of the mean of bird wing length (cm) samples (N = 40), with sampling repeated 1000 times.", out.width="90%"}
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
hist(x = all_means, col = "blue", cex.axis = 2, cex.lab = 2, main = "",
     xlab = "Sample mean of Wing length (cm)", breaks = 20,
     xlim = c(6.25, 7.75), ylim = c(0, 2.5), freq = FALSE);
mean_height <- 7;
arrows(x0 = mean_height, x1 = mean_height,
       y0 = 0.25, y1 = 0, lwd = 6, col = "red");
mbox <- function(x0, x1, y0, y1){
    xx <- seq(from=x0, to=x1, length.out = 100);
    yy <- seq(from=y0, to=y1, length.out = 100);
    xd <- c(rep(x0, 100), xx, rep(x1,100), rev(xx));
    yd <- c(yy, rep(y1,100), rev(yy), rep(y0, 100));
    return(list(x=xd, y=yd));
}
tbox <- mbox(x0 = mean_height - 0.2, 
             x1 = mean_height + 0.2, y0 = 0.25, y1 = 0.75);
polygon(x=tbox$x, y=tbox$y, lwd=3, border="red", col="white");
text(x = mean_height, y = 0.6, cex = 2, labels = "Population");
text(x = mean_height, y = 0.4, cex = 2, labels = "Mean");
```



## Sample means always distributed normally around true mean


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A histogram showing normally distributed data centered at 7.", fig.cap = "Distribution of the mean of bird wing length (cm) samples (N = 40), with sampling repeated 1000 times.", out.width="90%"}
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
hist(x = all_means, col = "blue", cex.axis = 2, cex.lab = 2, main = "",
     xlab = "Sample mean of Wing length (cm)", breaks = 20,
     xlim = c(6.25, 7.75), ylim = c(0, 2.5), freq = FALSE);
mean_height <- 7;
arrows(x0 = mean_height, x1 = mean_height,
       y0 = 0.25, y1 = 0, lwd = 6, col = "red");
mbox <- function(x0, x1, y0, y1){
    xx <- seq(from=x0, to=x1, length.out = 100);
    yy <- seq(from=y0, to=y1, length.out = 100);
    xd <- c(rep(x0, 100), xx, rep(x1,100), rev(xx));
    yd <- c(yy, rep(y1,100), rev(yy), rep(y0, 100));
    return(list(x=xd, y=yd));
}
tbox <- mbox(x0 = mean_height - 0.2, 
             x1 = mean_height + 0.2, y0 = 0.25, y1 = 0.75);
polygon(x=tbox$x, y=tbox$y, lwd=3, border="red", col="white");
text(x = mean_height, y = 0.6, cex = 2, labels = "Population");
text(x = mean_height, y = 0.4, cex = 2, labels = "Mean");

xx       <- seq(from = 5, to = 10, by = 0.001);
pr_norm  <- dnorm(x = xx, mean = 7, sd = sd(all_means));
points(x = xx, y = pr_norm, type = "l", lwd = 6, col = "red");
```




## Sample means always distributed normally around true mean


\Huge

- Distribution of sample means gets closer to the population mean with increasing $N$ \vspace{3mm}
- Can demonstrate this ourselves with simulated data



## Sample means always distributed normally around true mean


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A two panel figure, the first shows a barplot is shown with 11 bars corresponding to the probability of sampling 0-10 from a Poisson distribution with a rate parameter of 1. The second panel shows a normal distribution with data comprised of means sampled from the first panel.", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
lambda1pt0 <- dpois(x = 0:10, lambda = 1.0);
plot(x = 0, y = 0, xlim = c(0.5, 11.5), type = "n", ylim = c(0, 0.4), 
     xaxt = "n", ylab = "Probability", xlab = "Event (x)", 
     cex.lab = 2, cex.axis = 2, yaxs = "i");
axis(side = 1, at = 1:11, labels = 0:10, cex.axis = 2);
for(i in 1:11){
  yy <- seq(from = 0, to = lambda1pt0[i], length = 100);
  points(x = rep(i, times = 100), y = yy, type = "l", lwd = 6);  
}
lambda <- expression(paste(lambda, "= 1.0"));
text(x = 9, y = 0.37, cex = 2.5, labels = lambda);
clt_dat <- sample(x = 0:10, size = 1000 * 1000, replace = TRUE, 
                  prob = lambda1pt0);
clt_mat <- matrix(data = clt_dat, nrow = 1000, ncol = 1000);
clt_mns <- apply(X = clt_mat, MARGIN = 1, FUN = mean);
hist(x = clt_mns, xlab = expression(paste("Sample mean (",bar(x),")")), 
     main = "", cex.lab = 2, cex.axis = 2, breaks = 20, ylim = c(0, 145),
     xlim = c(0.82, 1.18), col = "blue");
```



## Sample means always distributed normally around true mean


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A two panel figure, the first shows a uniform probability distribution with a minimum of 1 and a maximum of 2.5. The second panel shows a normal distribution with data comprised of means sampled from the first panel.", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
alpha <- 1;
beta  <- 2.5;
yvals <- 1 / (2.5 - 1);
mbox <- function(x0, x1, y0, y1){
  xx <- seq(from=x0, to=x1, length.out = 100);
  yy <- seq(from=y0, to=y1, length.out = 100);
  xd <- c(rep(x0, 100), xx, rep(x1,100), rev(xx));
  yd <- c(yy, rep(y1,100), rev(yy), rep(y0, 100));
  return(list(x=xd, y=yd));
}
tbox <- mbox(x0 = alpha, x1 = beta, y0 = 0, y1 = yvals);
plot(x = 0, y = 0, type = "n", xlim = c(0, 3.5), ylim = c(0, 1),
     xlab = "Sample (x)", ylab = "Probability density",
     cex.lab = 2, cex.axis = 2, yaxs = "i");
polygon(x=tbox$x, y=tbox$y, lwd=3, border="black", col="blue");
clt_dat <- runif(n = 10000 * 1000, min = 1, max = 2.5);
clt_mat <- matrix(data = clt_dat, nrow = 1000, ncol = 10000);
clt_mns <- apply(X = clt_mat, MARGIN = 1, FUN = mean);
hist(x = clt_mns, xlab = expression(paste("Sample mean (",bar(x),")")), 
     main = "", cex.lab = 2, cex.axis = 2, breaks = 20, ylim = c(0, 120),
     xlim = c(1.73, 1.77), col = "blue");
```


## Probability density and the normal distribution

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A plot of a bell curve, with a range of the curve shaded in grey between negative sigma and positive sigma, symmetrical around the peak of the curve", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
xx       <- seq(from = -5, to = 5, by = 0.0001);
pr_norm  <- dnorm(x = xx, mean = 0, sd = 1);
plot(x = xx, y = pr_norm, type = "l", ylim = c(0, 0.48), xaxt = "n",
     ylab = "Probability density",  xlab = "x", lwd = 3, xlim = c(-3, 3),
     cex.lab = 2, cex.axis = 2, yaxs = "i");
xx_vals <- 40001:60001;
polygon(c(xx[xx_vals], xx[60001], xx[40001]), 
        c(pr_norm[xx_vals], 0, 0), col="blue");
yy_mean1 <- seq(from = 0, to = 0.15, length = 1000);
xx_mean1 <- rep(0, times = 1000);
yy_mean2 <- seq(from = 0.25, to = max(pr_norm), length = 1000);
xx_mean2 <- rep(0, times = 1000);
points(x = xx_mean1, y = yy_mean1, type = "l", lwd = 2, lty = "dotted");
points(x = xx_mean2, y = yy_mean2, type = "l", lwd = 2, lty = "dotted");
distr_lab <- c(expression(mu-sigma), expression(mu), expression(mu+sigma));
axis(side = 1, at = c(-1, 0, 1), labels = distr_lab, cex.axis = 2);
text(x = 0,  y = 0.2, labels = "68.2%", cex = 2);
```


## Probability density and the normal distribution


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A plot of a bell curve, with a range of the curve shaded in grey below an indicated value of 1.5 times sigma", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
xx       <- seq(from = -5, to = 5, by = 0.0001);
pr_norm  <- dnorm(x = xx, mean = 0, sd = 1);
plot(x = xx, y = pr_norm, type = "l", ylim = c(0, 0.48), xaxt = "n",
     ylab = "Probability density",  xlab = "x", lwd = 3, xlim = c(-3, 3),
     cex.lab = 2, cex.axis = 2, yaxs = "i");
xx_vals <- 1:65001;
polygon(c(xx[xx_vals], xx[65001], xx[1]), 
        c(pr_norm[xx_vals], 0, 0), col="blue");
yy_mean1 <- seq(from = 0, to = 0.15, length = 1000);
xx_mean1 <- rep(0, times = 1000);
yy_mean2 <- seq(from = 0.25, to = max(pr_norm), length = 1000);
xx_mean2 <- rep(0, times = 1000);
points(x = xx_mean1, y = yy_mean1, type = "l", lwd = 2, lty = "dotted");
points(x = xx_mean2, y = yy_mean2, type = "l", lwd = 2, lty = "dotted");
distr_lab <- c(expression(mu), expression(paste(mu, " + 1.5", sigma)));
axis(side = 1, at = c(0, 1.5), labels = distr_lab, cex.axis = 2);
text(x = 0,  y = 0.2, labels = "93.3%", cex = 2);
```



## Standard normal distribution

\Huge


\begin{itemize}
\item Need some standard values of $x$ to work out probability
\item Standard normal distribution
\begin{itemize} \Huge
\item Mean ($\bar{x}$) = 0
\item Standard deviation ($s$) = 1
\end{itemize}
\item Baseline for comparison
\end{itemize}


## Standard normal distribution

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A plot of a bell curve, shaded in grey and centered at the x-axis on a value of zero is shown. The x axis is labelled 'z-score'.", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
xx       <- seq(from = -5, to = 5, by = 0.001);
pr_norm  <- dnorm(x = xx, mean = 0, sd = 1);
plot(x = xx, y = pr_norm, type = "l", ylim = c(0, 0.48), 
     ylab = "Probability density",  xlab = "z-score", lwd = 3, xlim = c(-3, 3),
     cex.lab = 2, cex.axis = 2, yaxs = "i");
polygon(c(xx, max(xx)), c(pr_norm, 0), col="blue");
```


\footnotetext[1]{\href{https://bradduthie.github.io/stats/app/zandp/}{https://bradduthie.github.io/stats/app/zandp/}}



## Standard normal distribution

\Huge
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{z-score}: deviation from 0
\item Work out probability density
\item Basis for confidence intervals
\item Critical for hypothesis testing
\end{itemize}



## Translating a variable into a standard normal distribution

\Huge

- Subtract mean
- Divide by standard deviation

$$z = \frac{x - \bar{x}}{s}$$

\vspace{6mm}

Can turn x-values into z-scores



## Turning a value into z-score


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A histogram with normally distributed data.", out.width="90%"}
xvals <- c(7.53, 7.05, 8.11, 7.69, 7.81, 7.69, 7.33, 8, 8.20, 8.11, 8.08, 
           8.22, 8.18, 7.66, 7.82, 6.54, 8.04, 7.31, 7.51, 8.45, 7.58, 7.82, 
           8.03, 7.79, 7.95, 8.02); # Scotland temp 1995-2020 (degrees C)
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
hist(x = xvals, col = "blue", cex.axis = 2, cex.lab = 2, main = "",
     xlab = "Annual mean temperature in Scotland (degrees C)", breaks = 20,
     xlim = c(6.4, 8.6), ylim = c(0, 1.9), freq = FALSE);
```

\Large

$N = 26$ \hspace{6mm} $\bar{x} = 7.789$ \hspace{6mm} $s = 0.4124844$

\footnotetext[1]{Met Office. (July 1, 2021). Annual mean temperature in Scotland from 1995 to 2020. In Statista. Retrieved February 10, 2026, from \href{https://www.statista.com/statistics/367853/scotland-average-temperature/}{https://www.statista.com/statistics/367853/scotland-average-temperature/}}




## Turning a value into z-score


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A histogram with normally distributed data.", out.width="90%"}
xvals <- c(7.53, 7.05, 8.11, 7.69, 7.81, 7.69, 7.33, 8, 8.20, 8.11, 8.08, 
           8.22, 8.18, 7.66, 7.82, 6.54, 8.04, 7.31, 7.51, 8.45, 7.58, 7.82, 
           8.03, 7.79, 7.95, 8.02); # Scotland temp 1995-2020 (degrees C)
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
hist(x = xvals, col = "blue", cex.axis = 2, cex.lab = 2, main = "",
     xlab = "Annual mean temperature in Scotland (degrees C)", breaks = 20,
     xlim = c(6.4, 8.6), ylim = c(0, 1.9), freq = FALSE);

arrows(x0 = 6.540, x1 = 6.540, y0 = 0.6, y1 = 1/26*11, lwd = 5, col = "black");
mbox <- function(x0, x1, y0, y1){
    xx <- seq(from=x0, to=x1, length.out = 100);
    yy <- seq(from=y0, to=y1, length.out = 100);
    xd <- c(rep(x0, 100), xx, rep(x1,100), rev(xx));
    yd <- c(yy, rep(y1,100), rev(yy), rep(y0, 100));
    return(list(x=xd, y=yd));
}
tbox <- mbox(x0 = 6.540 - 0.1, 
             x1 = 6.540 + 0.15, y0 = 0.6, y1 = 0.9);
polygon(x=tbox$x, y=tbox$y, lwd=3, border="black", col="white");
text(x = 6.540 + 0.02, y = 0.75, cex = 2, labels = "6.54");
```

\Large

$N = 26$ \hspace{6mm} $\bar{x} = 7.789$ \hspace{6mm} $s = 0.4124844$

\footnotetext[1]{Met Office. (July 1, 2021). Annual mean temperature in Scotland from 1995 to 2020. In Statista. Retrieved February 10, 2026, from \href{https://www.statista.com/statistics/367853/scotland-average-temperature/}{https://www.statista.com/statistics/367853/scotland-average-temperature/}}




## Turning a value into z-score

\Huge

- Subtract mean
- Divide by standard deviation

$$z = \frac{6.54 - 7.789}{0.4124844} = -3.03$$

\vspace{6mm}

Over 3 standard deviations below the mean

## Turning a value into z-score


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A two panel figure, the first shows a curve of the standard normal distribution, to the right of which there is a histogram of data centred at a mean of 5 with a relatively high standard deviation. In the second figure, the histogram data fit nicely within the normal distribution", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
xx       <- seq(from = -5, to = 5, by = 0.001);
pr_norm  <- dnorm(x = xx, mean = 0, sd = 1);
hist(x = xvals, col = "blue", cex.axis = 2, cex.lab = 2, main = "",
     xlab = "Annual mean temperature in Scotland (degrees C)", breaks = 20,
     xlim = c(-4, 8.6), ylim = c(0, 1.9), freq = FALSE);
points(x = xx, y = pr_norm, type = "l", lty = "solid", lwd = 3);
arrows(x0 = 6, x1 = 1, y0 = 0.9, y1 = 0.3, lwd = 5, col = "black");
```





## Turning a value into z-score


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A two panel figure, the first shows a curve of the standard normal distribution, to the right of which there is a histogram of data centred at a mean of 5 with a relatively high standard deviation. In the second figure, the histogram data fit nicely within the normal distribution", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
zz   <- (xvals - 7.789)/0.4124844;
hist(x = zz, col = "blue", cex.axis = 2, cex.lab = 2, main = "",
     xlab = "Annual mean temperature in Scotland (degrees C)", breaks = 20,
     xlim = c(-4, 4), ylim = c(0, 1.9), freq = FALSE);
points(x = xx, y = pr_norm, type = "l", lty = "solid", lwd = 3);

arrows(x0 = -3.12, x1 = -3.12, y0 = 0.6, y1 = 0.225, lwd = 5, col = "black");
mbox <- function(x0, x1, y0, y1){
    xx <- seq(from=x0, to=x1, length.out = 100);
    yy <- seq(from=y0, to=y1, length.out = 100);
    xd <- c(rep(x0, 100), xx, rep(x1,100), rev(xx));
    yd <- c(yy, rep(y1,100), rev(yy), rep(y0, 100));
    return(list(x=xd, y=yd));
}
tbox <- mbox(x0 = -3.12 - 0.6, 
             x1 = -3.12 + 0.6, y0 = 0.6, y1 = 0.8);
polygon(x=tbox$x, y=tbox$y, lwd=3, border="black", col="white");
text(x = -3.12, y = 0.7, cex = 2, labels = "-3.03");
```



## Turning a value into z-score

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A two panel figure, the first shows a curve of the standard normal distribution, to the right of which there is a histogram of data centred at a mean of 5 with a relatively high standard deviation. In the second figure, the histogram data fit nicely within the normal distribution", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
xx       <- seq(from = -5, to = 5, by = 0.001);
pr_norm  <- dnorm(x = xx, mean = 0, sd = 1);
xvals    <- rnorm(n = 10000, mean = 5, sd = 2)
hist(xvals, main = "", freq = FALSE, breaks = 40, col = "blue", yaxs = "i",
     xlab = "x", xlim = c(-4, 12), ylim = c(0, 0.49), 
     ylab = "Probability density", cex.axis = 2, cex.lab = 2);
points(x = xx, y = pr_norm, type = "l", lty = "dotted", lwd = 3);
xstd <- (xvals - mean(xvals))/sd(xvals);
hist(xstd, main = "", freq = FALSE, breaks = 40, col = "blue", yaxs = "i",
     xlab = "x", xlim = c(-4, 12), ylim = c(0, 0.49), 
     ylab = "Probability density", cex.axis = 2, cex.lab = 2);
points(x = xx, y = pr_norm, type = "l", lty = "dotted", lwd = 3);
```





## Exploring distributions in jamovi with distrACTION module

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Screenshot of jamovi.", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/distraction.png")
```
